content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

November 3, 2015

Jeanne MacLeamy
Mayor, City of Novato
95 Alameda del Prado
Novato, CA 94949

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-15-186

Dear Ms. MacLeamy:

This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of interest or Section 1090.

QUESTION

Does the Act prohibit you from taking part in decisions to convey a 15-acre parcel to the City of Novato if your residential property will be assessed an annual fee of $2-10 for the maintenance of the property?

CONCLUSION

Because the decisions involve imposing an assessment that applies to your property, your interest is explicitly involved in the decision and the decision's effect on the property is foreseeable and material. Accordingly, you may not take part in the conveyance decisions.

FACTS

You are a member of the Novato City Council and its current mayor. You own and live in a residence in Novato. This residence is part of a neighborhood comprised of about 600 other homes. The property on which your residence is located is more than 1,500 feet away from a 15-acre piece of open space land owned by the Marin County Open Space District (the "MCOSD"). The MCOSD has a policy of not mowing the open space that it owns, including the 15-acre parcel.

¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-15-186
Page No. 2

Many of the 600 residents living in the neighborhood near to this 15-acre parcel are
concerned about the fire danger that MCOSD's refusal to mow this property presents, among other
things. A proposal has been made to convey this open space property to the City of Novato, which
does not have a policy against mowing open space property that it owns. It has also been proposed
that if the City accepts ownership of this 15-acre parcel, the approximately 600 neighbors would be
assessed for the costs incurred by the City in mowing this parcel. It is estimated that this cost would
be about $1,500 per year, or about $2-10/year per residential property. Your home would be
included in this assessment.

ANALYSIS

Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or using
his or her position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a financial interest.
(Section 87103.) A conflict of interest may arise only when the reasonably foreseeable financial
effect of a governmental decision on a public official's interests is material. Different standards
apply to determine whether a reasonably foreseeable financial effect on an interest will be material
depending on the nature of the interest. Interests from which a conflict of interest may arise are
defined in Section 87103.

Under Sections 82033 and 87103(b), an official has an interest in any real property owned
directly, indirectly, or beneficially by the public official or his or her immediate family, if the
interest has a fair market value of $2,000 or more. The only interest identified, which the decisions
in question may implicate, is your real property interest in your residence. Accordingly, we must
determine whether the financial effect on your residence resulting from the decisions is foreseeable
and material.

Generally, a financial effect is presumed to be reasonably foreseeable if the interest is
"explicitly involved" in the governmental decision. An interest is explicitly involved in a decision if
it is a named party in, or the subject of, a governmental decision before the official or the official's
agency. (Regulation 18701(a).) For an interest in real property, the interest is the subject of the
proceeding and thus "explicitly involved" in the decision anytime the decision "[w]ould impose,
repeal, or modify any taxes, fees, or assessments that apply to the parcel." (Regulations 18701(a)
and 18702.2(a)(3).) Likewise, any new tax, fee, or assessment on real property is considered a
material effect. (Regulation 18702.2(a)(3).)

Because decisions regarding the conveyance of the 15-acre parcels involve the imposition of
a new assessment on your interest in your residence, your interest is explicitly involved in the
decisions and the financial effect of the decisions on your property is both foreseeable and material.
Accordingly, you may not make, participate, or use your official position to influence the decisions.

File No. A-15-186
Page No. 3

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Hyla P. Wagner
General Counsel

By:    Brian G. Lau
       Counsel, Legal Division

BGL:jgl

and material. Accordingly, you may not take part in the conveyance decisions.
